era_context: The final year of the Warring States period in China, culminating in Qin Shihuang's unification of all major Chinese states. In the Mediterranean, the Second Punic War is in its early phase. The Hellenistic world remains fragmented among the successors of Alexander. In India, the Mauryan Empire is declining under weak successors to Ashoka.

events.title: Qin Shihuang completes unification of China
events.description: Qin Shihuang's forces complete the conquest of the last independent Warring States, particularly Qi. This marks the end of the Warring States period and the beginning of the Qin Dynasty as the first unified Chinese empire. Qin Shihuang adopts the title 'Huangdi' (Emperor) and establishes a centralized bureaucratic state. This unification fundamentally transforms Chinese political organization, establishing templates for imperial governance that persist for millennia. The standardization of weights, measures, writing systems, and currency follows.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Qin administrative records, bamboo slips, and oracle bones

events.certainty_note: The unification of China under Qin Shihuang in 221 BCE is one of the most securely dated events in ancient Chinese history, attested in multiple sources and corroborated by archaeological evidence. The Shiji provides detailed chronology, and administrative documents from the Qin period confirm the establishment of centralized governance.
cross_references: -213-001 (Burning of books and persecution of scholars)

events.title: Antiochus III accedes to Seleucid throne
events.description: Antiochus III (later known as 'the Great') becomes king of the Seleucid Empire following the death of his brother Seleucus II. He is approximately 20 years old and inherits a kingdom weakened by internal strife and loss of eastern territories. His reign (222–187 BCE) will be marked by ambitious military campaigns to restore Seleucid power, earning him the epithet 'the Great,' though he will ultimately face defeat by Rome at Magnesia (190 BCE).
events.certainty_note: Antiochus III's accession is well-documented in Polybius and later Greek and Roman sources. The date 222 BCE is secure, though some sources vary slightly.

events.title: Ptolemy III Euergetes dies; Ptolemy IV Philopator accedes
events.description: Ptolemy III Euergetes, who ruled Egypt for 25 years (246–221 BCE), dies and is succeeded by his son Ptolemy IV Philopator. Ptolemy IV's reign marks a decline in Ptolemaic power and stability. He is dominated by court factions and his reign is marked by internal strife, though he does achieve a notable military victory against Antiochus III at the Battle of Raphia (217 BCE). The transition of power is marked by the usual Ptolemaic court intrigues and the influence of powerful advisors.
key_figures: Arsinoe III (Queen, wife of Ptolemy IV)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemaic succession dates are well-established through papyrological evidence and Greek historical sources. Ptolemy III's death and Ptolemy IV's accession in 221 BCE are secure.

events.title: Second Punic War: Hannibal assumes command in Spain
events.description: Hannibal Barca, son of Hamilcar Barca, assumes command of Carthaginian forces in Spain following his father's death. Hannibal is approximately 26 years old and inherits a position of significant military authority. He begins consolidating Carthaginian power in Iberia and preparing for the conflict with Rome that will define his career. His assumption of command marks a turning point in the Second Punic War, as he will soon launch the famous campaign across the Alps into Italy.
events.certainty_note: Hannibal's assumption of command in Spain is attested in multiple classical sources, but the precise date is sometimes given as 221 or 220 BCE. Most modern scholars favor 221 BCE based on Polybius's chronology.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Qin Shihuang's unification to 221 BCE is based primarily on the Shiji, compiled centuries later. Some modern scholars debate whether the unification was truly complete in 221 BCE or whether it took several additional years; the conquest of Qi may have extended into 220 BCE. The Shiji's account is generally reliable but may conflate or simplify the timeline of conquest. For Hannibal's assumption of command, sources vary on whether this occurred in 221 or 220 BCE; Polybius is the most reliable source but was writing ~100 years after the events. Ptolemy IV's reign is often characterized as a period of decline, but recent scholarship has questioned whether this characterization is entirely fair, noting that he did maintain Egypt's territorial integrity and achieved military success at Raphia.
historiographic_note: Chinese sources are dominated by the Shiji, a Han-dynasty compilation with potential biases toward Han perspectives and possible anachronism. Archaeological evidence from Qin-period administrative documents provides corroboration but is fragmentary. For the Mediterranean, Polybius and Livy are primary sources, both written well after the events; Polybius is generally more reliable but both reflect Greco-Roman perspectives. Egyptian sources are primarily administrative papyri, which are abundant but often lack narrative context. The Seleucid Empire is documented primarily through Greek and later sources; Seleucid-era primary texts are sparse. All ancient sources on Hannibal are written by his enemies (Romans) or by later authors dependent on Roman sources, introducing potential bias.

graph_edges.note: Qin unification completes the consolidation begun in previous years
Antiochus III's accession initiates a period of Seleucid expansion leading to the Fourth Syrian War
Ptolemy IV's accession leads to his military confrontation with Antiochus III at Raphia
Hannibal's assumption of command in Spain leads to his crossing of the Alps
